name: Illinois Tollway (Toll Highway Authority)
tagline: A self-funded special district that runs 294 miles of all-electronic toll roads across northern Illinois — a tech-heavy operator whose tolling, ITS, and back-office systems make it one of the state's most technology-dependent agencies.
overview: The Illinois State Toll Highway Authority — the Illinois Tollway — is a self-supporting state special district that operates and maintains 294 miles of toll roads on five routes (I-90 Jane Addams, I-88 Reagan, I-355 Veterans, I-294/I-94 Tri-State, and the I-490/Elgin O'Hare Western Access corridor) across 12 northern Illinois counties. It is governed by an 11-member Board of Directors and run day-to-day by an Executive Director, with roughly 1,400 employees. Unlike most state agencies, the Tollway receives no general state tax revenue: it funds operations, maintenance, debt service, and capital almost entirely from toll collections and revenue bonds. That makes it unusually technology-dependent — the system is 100% all-electronic open-road tolling (cash collection ended in 2021), so I-PASS/E-ZPass transponder reads, license-plate (ANPR) cameras, the tolling back-office, intelligent transportation systems (ITS), and the operational technology (OT) along the roadway are mission-critical revenue and safety infrastructure rather than back-office IT. For a Dell SLG seller, the Tollway is a compute-, storage-, edge-, and cybersecurity-rich account whose buying is gated by board approval and the Illinois Procurement Code.

budget.intro: The Tollway's December 2025 board action set a 2026 budget of $1.79 billion in revenue covering operations, maintenance, debt service, and capital. Of that, $1.26 billion is capital and roughly $98 million is the Information Technology operating line — a sizable, self-funded technology spend that does not depend on state general revenue. A much larger commitment is queued: the proposed 15-year, $26.5B 'Driving Connections' program (2027–2042), which would be funded in part by the first toll increase since 2012.

stats.label: 2026 total budget
stats.note: Board-approved Dec 2025; operations, maintenance, debt service, capital

stats.label: 2026 capital
stats.note: Includes ~$374M systemwide roadway/bridge repair; funds Move Illinois / Bridging the Future

stats.label: 2026 IT operating
stats.note: Modernizing back-office systems and tolling/I-PASS support

notes: Self-funded model: no general state tax revenue — capital is backed by toll income plus revenue bonds (the Tollway planned up to $1B in new bonds in 2026).
The proposed 15-year, $26.5B 'Driving Connections' plan (2027–2042) would, if board-approved, expand the multi-year capital envelope well beyond current programs.
Treat exact line items as approximate — confirm against the latest adopted budget and consulting engineers' report before forecasting.

initiatives.name: All-electronic / open-road tolling back office
initiatives.summary: The Tollway runs 100% cashless open-road tolling on I-PASS/E-ZPass plus ANPR plate imaging. The transaction back-office, image processing, and customer systems are revenue-critical and continually modernized — a natural fit for high-availability compute, storage, data, and analytics infrastructure.

initiatives.name: ITS and roadway OT
initiatives.summary: Intelligent transportation systems, traffic management, sensors, cameras, and roadside operational technology run the length of the 294-mile system. This edge/OT footprint drives needs in ruggedized edge compute, networking, and OT-aware security.

initiatives.name: IT and back-office modernization
initiatives.summary: The 2026 budget funds modernization of back-office and business systems supporting I-PASS and open-road tolling, with an ~$98M IT operating line. Refresh cycles span servers, storage, virtualization, and data platforms.

initiatives.name: Cybersecurity and resilience
initiatives.summary: As critical transportation infrastructure with 24/7 monitoring of its systems and networks, the Tollway must defend both IT and OT environments. The IT/OT convergence trend across toll and transit operators points to growing demand for segmentation, monitoring, and resilient infrastructure.

procurement.intro: The Tollway buys under the Illinois Procurement Code, and contract awards require Board of Directors approval. Non-construction goods and services (where most IT/technology buys sit) flow through state procurement systems; construction and professional engineering follow separate IDOT/CDB and QBS rules. Vendors register and transact through the state's electronic procurement tooling.

vehicles.name: Illinois Procurement Code (goods & services)
vehicles.detail: Non-construction supplies and services — including IT hardware, software, and services — are solicited and awarded under the state procurement code and JCAR rules. This is the primary path for technology buys.

vehicles.name: BidBuy / Illinois Procurement Gateway (IPG)
vehicles.detail: State e-procurement platforms: vendors register and complete required forms in the IPG and respond to solicitations and notices of award posted in BidBuy.

vehicles.name: Board of Directors approval
vehicles.detail: Award recommendations must be approved by the 11-member Tollway Board; bidders are notified after the board acts. Board cadence and committee review shape deal timing.

powerStructure.name: Cassaundra Rouse
powerStructure.title: Executive Director
powerStructure.note: Top executive over the 294-mile system and ~1,400 staff; owns day-to-day direction and the relationship that anchors any strategic motion.

powerStructure.name: Michael Shay
powerStructure.title: Chief Information Officer
powerStructure.note: Inaugural CIO; leads Information Technology and Business Systems — the central buying center for compute, storage, data, tolling back-office, and IT modernization.

powerStructure.name: Cathy Williams
powerStructure.title: Chief Financial Officer
powerStructure.note: Oversees financial operations and the agency's ~$15B long-term financial plan; key on capital funding and bond-backed program spend.

powerStructure.name: Information security function (under IT)
powerStructure.title: Cybersecurity / CISO role
powerStructure.note: The Tollway runs 24/7 security monitoring, but a CISO is not publicly named as a separate executive — security appears to sit within IT under the CIO. Confirm the current owner directly.

powerStructure.name: Board of Directors (11 members)
powerStructure.title: Governing body
powerStructure.note: Approves contracts, budgets, and capital programs. Governor-appointed; the gate every significant award must clear.

news.title: Tollway proposes 15-year, $26.5B 'Driving Connections' capital plan with first toll hike since 2012
news.impact: If board-approved, a multi-year capital surge (2027–2042) funded by ~45-cent passenger and ~30% commercial toll increases starting Jan 1, 2027 — a long-horizon expansion of program spend and technology demand.

news.title: Board approves $1.79B 2026 budget with $1.26B in capital
news.impact: Sets the spending envelope, including the ~$98M IT operating line and back-office/tolling modernization — the near-term budget Dell opportunities map to.

news.title: 2026 program advances O'Hare access via Elgin O'Hare Western Access (I-490)
news.impact: Major corridor build-out adds ITS, tolling, and roadside OT scope across new infrastructure.

dellActions: Map the IT buying center under CIO Michael Shay and lead with high-availability compute, storage, and data infrastructure for the all-electronic tolling back office.
Position edge and ruggedized infrastructure plus OT-aware security for the 294-mile ITS and roadside operational-technology footprint.
Confirm Dell's standing on the relevant Illinois Procurement Code / BidBuy vehicles and ensure vendor registration in the IPG so awards can flow without delay.
Time engagement to the board calendar and the FY2026 IT/modernization line — and track the proposed $26.5B 'Driving Connections' plan as a multi-year pipeline if it clears the board.
Clarify who owns cybersecurity (CISO function under IT) and bring an IT/OT convergence story — segmentation, monitoring, and resilient infrastructure for critical revenue and safety systems.
